questions.question: Define the difference between static and instance methods in Java.
keyPoints: Static methods belong to the class and can be called without creating an instance.
Instance methods belong to an object and require an instance to be called.

questions.question: Explain the concept of pass-by-value in Java for both primitive types and objects.
keyPoints: Java uses pass-by-value for all parameters, including primitives and objects.
For primitive types, a copy of the value is passed to the method.
For objects, a copy of the reference is passed to the method.

questions.question: Compare and contrast static and instance methods in Java. Provide examples to illustrate the differences.
keyPoints: Static methods are shared by all instances of a class, while instance methods are unique to each instance.
Static methods can only access static variables, while instance methods can access both static and instance variables.
An example of a static method is a utility method that performs a calculation, while an example of an instance method is a method that updates an object's state.

questions.question: Explain the concept of method overloading in Java. Provide an example of how method overloading can be used to improve code readability and maintainability.
keyPoints: Method overloading is the process of declaring multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists.
Method overloading can be used to improve code readability and maintainability by allowing multiple methods to share the same name.

An example of method overloading is a class that has multiple methods with the same name but different parameters, such as a method that takes a single integer parameter and another method that takes two integer parameters.

questions.question: Discuss the importance of access control in Java. Explain how access control can be used to restrict access to methods and variables.
keyPoints: Access control is used to restrict access to methods and variables in Java.
Access control can be used to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data and methods.
Java provides several access control modifiers, including public, private, and protected, which can be used to control access to methods and variables.
